1: Welcome home. This is a calm walkthrough about the first stretch with your newborn, the very first weeks at home. A lot will feel new, and you get to take it entirely at your own pace.
2: We talk about the newborn baby, about sleep, crying, and comfort, about care, nappies, and poo, about jaundice and safe sleep, about when you should seek care, and about Jocke's role and how you look after yourselves. Stop and ask whenever you like.
3: A newborn often looks a little different from what you imagined. They can be puffy, have some fine downy hair and flaky skin, and small spots that pass on their own. Your baby is born with reflexes: it roots for the breast, grips your finger tightly, and sometimes flings out its arms. Its sight is best at twenty to thirty centimetres, exactly your face at the breast, and it recognises your voice and your scent from before.
4: Newborns sleep a lot, but in short stretches, and they have no day or night rhythm yet. Your baby sleeps, wakes for food usually every two to four hours, is awake a short while, and falls back to sleep. Day and night are the same for your baby right now. A dark, calm night and a bright, active day help the rhythm find itself eventually.
5: Crying is not something wrong, it is your baby's only language. It says hunger, tiredness, a nappy that needs changing, or just hold me. Four tricks often help: swaddle your baby snugly, hold them on the side or tummy against your arm, make a steady shhh sound near the ear, and let them suck on the breast, a finger, or a dummy while you rock gently. If the crying cannot be soothed and your baby seems ill or listless, look at the section on when you should seek care.
6: The everyday care is usually simpler than it sounds. At a nappy change you wipe from front to back, let the bottom air for a while, and change often. Keep the umbilical stump dry and clean, and it falls off on its own after about one to two weeks. A bath is rarely needed in the beginning, lukewarm water once a week or so is plenty.
7: The nappies are your best receipt that your baby is getting enough, and they change clearly in the first days. The first one or two days there is only the odd wet nappy and a black, sticky stool. Day three to four there are more wet nappies and the stool shifts towards green. From day five onwards you want to see six or more wet nappies a day and a yellow, loose stool. For few wet nappies, dark stool after the first week, or blood, get in touch with the child health centre (BVC).
8: A lot that looks dramatic in a newborn is completely normal and passes on its own. Jaundice is common a few days after birth and usually harmless, and the BVC keeps track of it. Your baby sneezes, hiccups, and clears its throat to clear the nose and airways, this is not a cold. It is often noisy in its sleep, whimpering and grunting, and may jerk with shaky movements. But if the jaundice increases or your baby becomes very yellow or listless, contact care the same day.
9: A few simple habits make sleep as safe as possible, at every nap and around the clock. Always put your baby to sleep on the back. Use a flat, firm mattress with the sheet properly fastened, and keep the bed bare, without a pillow, duvet, soft toy, or blanket near the face. Let your baby sleep in its own bed next to you for the first months, comfortably cool and smoke-free all around.
10: A newborn cannot tell you how it feels, so trust your gut, once too often rather than too seldom. Crying that can be soothed, sneezing, hiccups, and noises in sleep are everyday. Contact the BVC or care the same day if the jaundice increases, if your baby feeds more and more sluggishly, whimpers persistently, or has fewer wet nappies. And a fever in a newborn, call straight away, likewise for refusing to feed, a listless and hard-to-wake baby, or breathing difficulties.
11: Jocke, the first weeks are just as much about protecting Felicia as about caring for the baby. Take the nights, change, burp, and comfort so that Felicia can sleep between feeds. Nappies and carrying you do just as well. Screen off visits and errands so the first weeks can be calm, and take skin to skin yourself too, entirely on your own terms.
12: Don't forget that you need care too. A rested and well-fed pair of parents can cope with more, and your care for each other is care for the baby too. Sleep when your baby sleeps, eat and drink simply and often, and ask for help, say yes to food, laundry, and a stretch of sleep. You don't have to do everything right, just be there, over and over. That is enough.
13: You find your rhythm, one day at a time. Trust your baby, trust yourselves, and ask for help early. Get in touch with your questions, we take them together.
